Q1: I usually organized my study routine around my reading and writing preferences, so I'd set aside specific blocks during the week to go through course materials and take detailed notes. I'd typically log into the LMS in the mornings when my mind felt clearest, download what I needed, and then work through it methodically. I tried to keep things consistent even when I wasn't feeling great emotionally. Having a routine helped me stay on track despite everything going on in my head.
Q2: I definitely tried to study at regular times each week because that structure really helped me stay grounded. However, my attendance wasn't always consistent with classes themselves, so I compensated by being more disciplined with my independent study time. When deadlines approached, I'd increase my study hours, but I always had that baseline routine I could fall back on. It gave me a sense of control.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS first thing in the morning as part of my daily routine, almost like checking off a box on my to-do list. Sometimes it was to check if new materials were posted or to see discussion updates. Other times I just needed to feel connected to the course even if I wasn't physically attending lectures. It became a habit more than anything else.
Q4: Yes, logging into the LMS definitely became part of my normal routine after the first few weeks. I think it happened because I set a specific time each day to check it, and eventually it just felt automatic. The mindfulness exercises I'd been doing helped me build better habits overall, and checking the LMS became one of those anchoring activities in my day. It gave me structure when other things felt uncertain.
Q5: The written materials and reading resources were most useful to me since I learn best through reading and writing. I also found the discussion forums helpful because I could engage at my own pace and think through my responses carefully. The videos were good too, though I often preferred to read transcripts when they were available. Having everything organized in one place made it easier to plan my study sessions.
Q6: I tended to ignore some of the optional interactive activities because they felt less essential to my learning style. I also sometimes delayed watching videos if I knew I could get the same information from the readings. Without reliable internet access at home, I had to be strategic about what I engaged with online versus what I could download and work through offline. Some resources just didn't fit into my workflow.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when it clearly connected to the assignments or exams and when I could see how it would help me understand the material better. If I could read or write about it and really process the information, that made it valuable to me. I also appreciated activities that I could complete at my own pace without needing to be online constantly. Practical relevance was key.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was less pressure attached to them, which sometimes made them easier to skip. Required activities had clear deadlines and consequences, so I prioritized those even when I wasn't feeling motivated. With optional ones, I'd only engage if I genuinely found them interesting or thought they'd help with assignments. The lack of structure around them made it easier to let them slide.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wasn't visible in the LMS. I'd download readings and PDFs, then work through them offline with handwritten notes since I don't have reliable internet access at home. I also used textbooks and my own summary documents extensively. A lot of my learning happened away from the screen, just me and my notebook working through concepts.
Q10: There were many times when I'd spend hours reviewing my notes or re-reading downloaded materials without any LMS activity showing. I'd also sometimes study from printed handouts or work through practice problems on paper. My best studying often happened in quiet moments with my written notes spread out around me. The LMS wouldn't capture any of that depth of engagement.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access materials once, download everything I needed, and then continue studying them offline for days or even weeks. Without consistent internet, this was the most practical way for me to learn. I'd return to the LMS mainly to check for new content or submit assignments. Most of my actual learning time happened disconnected from the platform.
Q12: There were moments when I felt sad and it became harder to engage with everything, including the course. During those times, even logging in felt like an effort, and I'd just do the bare minimum to keep up. The mindfulness practices helped me work through those periods without completely falling behind. I learned to be gentle with myself while still maintaining some level of routine.
Q13: Fatigue definitely affected how I used the LMS, especially when I was feeling emotionally low. On those days, I'd log in but maybe not engage as deeply with the materials. Competing responsibilities sometimes meant I had to rush through things rather than taking my time. But overall, I tried to maintain my routine even when it was hard, because the structure itself was helpful for my wellbeing.
Q14: Yes, there were times when I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I was just going through the motions because I felt like I should be doing something productive. Those moments happened more when I was feeling sad or overwhelmed. I'd later have to go back and actually engage with the content properly.
Q15: I did postpone some activities, particularly when my mood was low and I couldn't muster the energy to engage meaningfully. I knew they were important, but sometimes I just needed to take care of myself first. The mindfulness approach I'd been practicing taught me that pushing through wasn't always the answer. I'd come back to things when I felt more capable of actually absorbing the information.
Q16: Deadlines were really important motivators for me and shaped when I'd intensify my LMS usage. I'd plan backwards from due dates and make sure I had everything downloaded and ready well in advance. As deadlines approached, I'd log in more frequently to double-check requirements and submission details. They gave my routine a sense of purpose and urgency when I needed it.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my engagement because it showed me whether my approach was working. When I received good marks on assignments, it reinforced my study methods and motivated me to continue. Constructive feedback helped me adjust my focus for future work. I appreciated timely feedback because it kept me connected to the course even when I wasn't attending lectures regularly.
Q18: The structure of the course worked well for me because materials were organized clearly and I could plan my study sessions around them. Having everything in modules made it easy to work through content systematically. The clear expectations for assignments helped me manage my time despite not attending many lectures. I appreciated knowing exactly what was required of me.
Q19: The availability of downloadable materials made engagement much easier given my internet limitations. Having discussion forums where I could participate asynchronously also helped. What made things harder was when activities required real-time participation or constant connectivity. I wished there were more text-based alternatives to video content for accessibility reasons.
Q20: I think students might show high activity because they're trying to engage but struggling to produce work that meets their standards. They might also be dealing with personal issues that make it hard to complete assignments even when they're putting in effort. Sometimes exploring materials extensively is part of the learning process even if it doesn't translate to submissions. It doesn't necessarily mean they're not trying.
Q21: This definitely reflects my experience somewhat. My attendance was low and I did a lot of studying offline, so my LMS activity might not fully represent my engagement. I performed reasonably well because I was diligent about working through materials on my own time and in my own way. The LMS activity is just one piece of the puzzle when it comes to understanding how a student is actually learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like login frequency and submission timing, which indicate routine and consistency. But they miss the quality of engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture whether someone was really absorbing information or just clicking through. They also miss the emotional and personal context that affects how someone engages with their studies.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely connecting with the material and caring about understanding it, not just completing tasks. For me, it involves active reading, thoughtful note-taking, and reflecting on how concepts fit together. It's about being mentally present even when you're studying alone. True engagement goes beyond what any tracking system can measure—it's an internal state of curiosity and commitment.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with printed materials and handwritten notes, which is where most of my real learning happened. It also wouldn't capture the emotional effort it took to maintain my routine when I was feeling sad, or how the mindfulness practices helped me stay resilient. My engagement was deeply personal and internal, and the data only shows the surface-level interactions.
